data: Small, damaged 'pseudo-aedicola', of the coarse local stone, coated with stucco and painted; text incised.
Incised and painted, one word per line within a recessed frame (H 14.5 cm x W 13.5 cm), stucco on stone.
Excavations 1974, via del Fante, adjoining Cooperativa S. Francesco
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2023-07-18
Museo archeologico regionale Lilibeo Marsala - Baglio Anselmi
Comprehensive collection of Archaeological material discovered at the site of Lilybaeum and in the surrounding area
Lilibeo. Testimonianze archeologiche dal IV sec a.C. al V sec. d.C.
C.A. Di Stefano, Lilibeo. Testimonianze archeologiche dal IV sec a.C. al V sec. d.C. (Palermo, 1984)
Su alcune iscrizioni inedite di Marsala
M.T. Manni Piraino, «Su alcune iscrizioni inedite di Marsala», in Studi di storia antica offerti dagli allievi a Eugenio Manni (Rome: Giorgio Bretschneider, 1976), 213–22
Lilibeo e il Mare. Il museo archeologico regionale di Marsala

Enrico Caruso e Maria Grazia Griffo, a c. di, Lilibeo e il Mare. Il museo archeologico regionale di Marsala (Palermo: Regione Siciliana, Assessorato dei Beni Culturali e dell’identita siciliana, 2024)

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text of Brugnone2024, checked against photograph ;
  • 2: Brugnone: alternatively Ποπλείο[ς]

translation

Damatrios son of Popleios, blessed one, rejoice!

commentary

The formula μακαρῖτα χαῖρε is found in another funerary epitaph at Lilybaeum, ISic003660. The second name, Popleios (i.e. Latin Publius) could either be a patronymic, or else the adoption of a Latin name as a second name, as attested in other Sicilian cases (Cic. Verr. 2.4.35, 2.5.16).

Δαμάτριος Ποπλείου μακαρῖτα χαῖρη
Text of Brugnone2024, checked against photograph Brugnone: alternatively Ποπλείο[ς]

Damatrios son of Popleios, blessed one, rejoice!

The formula μακαρῖτα χαῖρε is found in another funerary epitaph at Lilybaeum, ISic003660. The second name, Popleios (i.e. Latin Publius) could either be a patronymic, or else the adoption of a Latin name as a second name, as attested in other Sicilian cases (Cic. Verr. 2.4.35, 2.5.16).
